locomo_experiment_user_4.question: What kind of yoga for building core strength might John benefit from?
locomo_experiment_user_4.answer:
Given John’s goals (using yoga to boost strength, flexibility, focus, and balance — Dec 1, 2023) and that he already enjoys standing power poses like Warrior II, these options will best build core strength:

Recommended yoga styles
- Power Vinyasa / Power Yoga (dynamic flows with plank/chaturanga work)
- Ashtanga (structured, core‑intensive primary series)
- Baptiste or “core-focused” yoga classes
- Yoga + Pilates fusion (emphasizes targeted core work)

High‑impact poses and progressions to include
- Plank and forearm plank (builds overall core stability)
- Side plank (Vasisthasana) and side plank variations (oblique strength)
- Boat pose (Navasana) and half‑boat holds/leg lifts (hip flexors and deep core)
- Chaturanga → Upward dog → Plank flows (dynamic core endurance)
- Warrior III and Chair pose (Utkatasana) — standing balance + core control
- Revolved chair and twisting boat (obliques)
- Dolphin pose and dolphin plank (shoulder + core)
- Locust and bridge variations (posterior chain support for the core)

Simple sample mini‑sequence (20–30 min)
- Warm up: sun salutations (5 minutes)
- Core flow: plank → chaturanga → up‑dog → plank (3–5 rounds, controlled)
- Strength holds: 3 × 30–60s plank, 3 × 30s side plank each side
- Balance + power: 3 × 30s Warrior III each side, 3 × 30s Chair holds (add twists)
- Floor finish: 3 × 20–30s boat variations, bridge/locust for posterior chain
- Cool down & breath work

Training plan tips (fits his schedule and recovery approach)
- 2–3 yoga sessions per week (20–40 minutes focused on core) complements his strength training and basketball
- Gradually increase hold times and add dynamic reps; track progress
- Prioritize form to protect lower back; modify (bend knees in boat, reduce hold time) if needed
- Use “power vinyasa core” or “yoga for athletes/core strength” classes when searching

Because John already values balancing workouts and listening to his body (Aug 21, 2023), these styles and the above sequence should directly strengthen his core while supporting his basketball and strength goals.

locomo_experiment_user_4.question: What other exercises can help John with his basketball performance?
locomo_experiment_user_4.answer:
From the memories, John already uses strength training and daily physical‑therapy exercises and has a balanced workout plan. To complement that and boost basketball performance (explosiveness, injury prevention, conditioning, shooting), he could add:

- Plyometrics — builds explosiveness for jumping and quick first steps (box jumps, depth jumps, tuck jumps).  
- Agility and change‑of‑direction drills — improves lateral quickness and on‑court cutting (ladder drills, cone T‑drills, shuttle runs).  
- Sprint/conditioning work — basketball‑specific conditioning for repeated high‑intensity efforts (court sprints, suicides, HIIT intervals).  
- Unilateral strength and stability — reduces injury risk and improves balance for drives and landings (single‑leg RDLs, split squats, step‑ups).  
- Core and trunk work — enhances power transfer and shooting stability (planks, Pallof press, Russian twists).  
- Mobility and flexibility work — preserves joint health and range of motion (hip/ankle mobility drills, dynamic warmups, yoga).  
- Sport‑specific skill work — high‑rep shooting under fatigue and situational drills to raise shooting percentage (catch‑and‑shoot reps, pull‑up jumpers, late‑game scenarios).  
- Continue daily physical‑therapy/recovery routines — to manage injury risk and sustain training volume.

Keep these integrated into his current schedule (as he already balances strength work and basketball), prioritize recovery, and progress gradually while listening to his body.
